author: @boogieknee3781
text:
Thankyou rob.For doing some episodes on upcycling and laying off from the expensive toys for a change.
My neighbours and I like to upcycle stuff and repair and give away those items that too many folk just bin.
I get a lot of satisfaction from offering the item back to the original owners after stripping down an item and fixing it after they had given it up as "knackered".

author: @beamer.electronics
text: In electronics, I use a used toothbrush (head cut-off and shaped to a point) to physically tap boards for intermittent connections and dry joints.

author: @reincarnation-milk
text: They have been used for decades for cleaning.  Often to be found in a potentially carsagenic pool of oily petrol in a 1960s roasting tin in the back of your dad's garage.

author: @rayadagio
text: Great invention!! Though I have slight doubts as to whether this also works well with used brush heads, since the bristles are usually worn and bent and the force is therefore not transmitted as well. But new tooshbrush heads are also available separately without a handle, so you can use new brushes without producing additional waste.

author: @Michael-vp4zt
text: Here's the deal- these things came out of a mine in the ground... why not just put them back? You say they take a thousand years to break down. So what? How long does a piece of coal last in the same hole? Just don't put them in your fish tank and they'll be fine.

replies.author: @rayadagio
replies.text: Oh my god!

author: @michaelvaughn7137

author: @leslieapplegate65
text: I save them for my veterinarian and she uses them to scrub tattoo ink in goat ears.

replies.author: @retromodernart4426
replies.text: And as anyone who's watched one of those prisoner diaries type  series knows, she could stick a needle in the handle of the toothbrush and do the tattooing with it.

replies.author: @leslieapplegate65
replies.text: @retromodernart4426 We usually use a set of tattoo pliers to do goats. You put the digits in the pliers, smear some (usually green) ink on them and tattoo the ear. Then scrub the ink in. It results in a pretty long lasting mark. No one wants to re-do an adult goat....

replies.author: @retromodernart4426
replies.text: @leslieapplegate65 Thanks for the interesting info!

author: @timothyneiswander3151

author: @ksgraham3477
text:
I really detest these new multi-material toothbrushes. The handles get gummy feeling with age to leverage the purchase of a new toothbrush, even if the bristles are still in good shape.
The old plastic handled ones were so much nicer!
Also, try to find a firm bristle brush these days!

author: @gosekinz
text: Mechanical rectifiers .. maybe Theo Jansen's Strandbeests?? those wind-walker creatures highly recommended watching

author: @Nick_Tag
text: My favourite fun fact about the toothbrush is that the idea for them came to Addis (that tupperware company) while he was in jail for starting a riot at Spitalfields Market. He was watching the guards sweeping up or something, back then folks used a cloth. And it took 10 years to actually commercialise the idea.

author: @Nick_Tag
text: I like the overrun protection idea -- and have something a bit wacky to contribute. [Of course it would wash off in the rain but] try putting some toothpaste in between two interlocking toothbrushes and try having a tug of war match with Luke.. it's a surprising amount of strength. (Maybe I learnt that from this channel actually, I can't remember now!)

author: @brettbridger362
text: Great as usual. Just to clarify, when you talk about using it as part of a overrun device, I'm assuming you're putting something like a governor on the shaft, so if the turbine starts to be driven too fast, it will either disengage the clutch or slow the shaft. If it slows the shaft, the bristles would be forced to 'skip', which would eventually ruin the bristles, forcing you to use some of the remaining 199,999,990 odd you have left to replace them. Am I understanding you correctly? Thanks in advance.

author: @HealthyDisrespectforAuthority
text: Have you got any thoughts on up-cycling empty C02 cartridges used for making sparkling water? or the Nitrogen ones for whipped cream?

replies.author: @brettbridger362
replies.text: Probably the biggest issue is size. Assuming they are still made out of steel, like they use to be, I can't see they're useful for much as they are too easy to recycle. If they float when empty, you could use them as a float for fishing, if they don't, use them as sinkers? Attach some fins and use them like a compressed air toy rocket? Take the tops off and use them as shot glasses?

author: @howardg396
text: We collect toothbrushes in our lab, and use them for detail cleaning of our fossils. This does yea down the bristles but leave us with the handles. How would one convert the handles into filament? You mentioned chopping them up, but what would the next steps be?

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: There’s loads of videos on it. Basically you shred the plastic and then feed it into an injection extrusion apparatus that squeezes it out “like toothpaste” at a desired diameter and feeds it onto a roll.

Sending love, will always watch one of your videos! I hope to make it to the UK and have the opportunity to visit 🤞🏼🤞🏼

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text: If you're really worried about a smelly toothbrush, then let it soak in a bleach solution before storage for eventual re-use/recycling.

author: @DirkLarien
text: back when i used to visit pubs and smoke. I noticed how much buts bunch of us produced during the evening. And spat out ridiculous idea that since each pub has to be making mountains of cigarette buts. What if we were to make something out of em. Like house insulation. Obviously washing them in some solution to get rid of the smell. I bet pub owners would give them out free if you were to collect em every now and then. Everyone laughed at me athe time. And i kid you not couple years later i saw an news article of a guy few miles south doing just that.

replies.author: @malcolm8564
replies.text: Better to stop people from smoking.

replies.author: @DirkLarien

They're really great for cleaning other things.
The grouting on tiles, and you use less cleaner too.
With parafin/gunk/ WD40, mechanical and electrical components and those hard to reach places on a car for example.

author: @screen-protector
text: I use used toothbrushes to clean flux and dust in my electronics repair shop. We don't waste them ;) more than maybe 1 per year ;) here on a whole family.

author: @af0ulwind
text: Anything... and i mean anything can be recycled... try putting it in a juicer remove all oxygen and run the drain hose through a bubbler and out over several containers with condensate plates hanging into them.... you will heat the juicer and the plastics and organics will gasify. the gas will come out through the drain and bubble up through the bubbler and condensate at different plates and drip into containers of different fuels/oils/gasses

author: @locouk
text:
I do pat testing at work atm, I’ve noticed that hair curlers and straighteners have like a slip-ring set up on the handle/ cable so the 230 volt lead can be turned constantly in the same direction without any tangles. 
  It must be a very reliable system and I’m wondering if it can be used for wind turbines?

author: @401ksolar
text: I worked on a factory robot station that would melt hot glue in a large pot and then the hot glue robot would use the melted hot glue from the pot to glue wherever it needed to, it seems like this method could be used on those 3D printers instead of using filament you could just toss the handles into the pot and let it heat up for a couple of hours?

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: there are large format pellet fed auger machines...  part way there (melting the raw pellets "just in time" - no need for pre formed filament (basically making it in a continuous process)

author: @thelatemickb6927
text: So lets buy brand new toothbrushes to demonstrate how terrible they are. 8 of them is 4 years for me. I'd like a small nuclear reactor to prove a similar point.

replies.author: @martinsmallridge4025
replies.text:
Ideally Rob would use used toothbrushes but he has a platform that encourages thousands, if not more to consider ways to upcycle the things.

I do the exact same thing with inkjet printers where I know the use of a few new printers in fix-it demos has resulted in literally tens of thousands of tons of unrealised electronic waste because of a small investment up front.

Maybe consider this as an investment instead of nitpicking from a narrow viewpoint?

author: @aaronsj80
text: What processes lead to the toothbrushes ending up in the ocean in the first place? Obviously people aren't just throwing them into the ocean. So after trash pickup, are they just dumping it into the ocean? Are the landfills placed in such a way that trash washes into the ocean? Is it a regional thing where some countries handle the yeah better than other countries? I think that questions need to be answered before blanking the product for the problem.

replies.author: @WilliamTythas
replies.text: we sell trash to other countries and they don't have environmental codes and are just dumping it in the ocean

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @WilliamTythas  - ahh yes, pass off the hard jobs to the poor...  Times haven't really changed in the world - the village just got bigger.  (Include myself in this mess).

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text:
I visited Madeira years ago,  with the tourists industry large amounts of plastic waste was produced and I enquired about it   It was taken out to sea and dumped,  this is willful vandalism there are plenty of viable solutions 
Waste is an unused resource. If we come to our senses one day we will be mining our landfills

replies.text: @kadmow Flame retardants are a good thing ..... but if your walls melt at 250 degrees you may find walls sagging rather than burning to be a problem

replies.author: @WilliamTythas
replies.text: I'm guessing this would take a long, long time given the forces involved in a microgenerator like suggested in the video, better would be a little armature that would hold the heads not glue .. so when they wore down you could replace them .. as you can tell all these brushes he used were new ...
